speaker: narrator
text: Nyxara was already awake when the light arrived; warlocks don't court daylight -- they wait it out. She came down the loft's shadow-ladder of the stable and stopped in the dark beneath the last rafter, away from the door. The left glove came off.

speaker: narrator
text: She held the hand up in the thin grey light coming through the gap in the boards. The fingertips were black to the second knuckle now. The skin had the texture of something that had been cold too long -- not dead, not yet, but learning the direction.

speaker: narrator
text: She flexed each finger in turn, feeling the nerves report from their diminished distance. Not pain exactly. More like the memory of pain, arriving late and quiet. She counted the joints. Same number as yesterday.

speaker: narrator
text: She had stopped being grateful for that and started just counting.

speaker: narrator
text: The ring finger was the worst.

speaker: narrator
text: The black had climbed past the second knuckle to the base, and when she bent it, there was a fraction of a second's delay between the instruction and the obedience -- a tiny lag, like a conversation through a translator, where her body had once been a single language.

speaker: narrator
text: She'd noticed the delay three days ago. She hadn't told anyone. The delay was hers.

speaker: narrator
text: She tested the grip: fist closed, fist open, fist closed. Strength at maybe seventy percent. Enough for wards. Enough for the subtle work -- the suggestion-veils, the psychic caltrops she'd laid during the siege. Not enough for the deep casting, the patron's full gift. Not without losing more.

speaker: narrator
text: The math was simple and she'd done it many times. Each major casting cost a joint. A knuckle. An inch of herself converted to the patron's currency. At this rate, the hand by winter. The arm by spring. And after the arm --

speaker: narrator
text: She didn't think past the arm. Past the arm was a conversation she wasn't ready to have.

speaker: narrator
text: The glove went back on before she stepped into the yard.

speaker: narrator
text: She crossed the square alone. The cobbles were still scorched in places where Caelin's fire had swept through; the barricade had been pulled aside but not yet cleared, and someone had left a bucket where a man had died.

speaker: narrator
text: The smell of the night before hung in the cold air -- pitch, iron, the strange wrong-sweetness the thralls left behind. She walked through it without slowing. She had learned early that the best way to move through a bad place was to move through it.

speaker: narrator
text: She found Caelin where she'd expected -- on the stable step in the early cold, carefully re-wrapping his arm, testing the grip against the ache. He didn't try to stand when she approached. It would cost too much.

speaker: narrator
text: A thumb-sized mirror-coin lay in her palm, frost veining the face from the inside.

speaker: unknown
text: "Summoned,"

speaker: narrator
text: she said.

speaker: unknown
text: "And I was told to tell you. You, specifically."

speaker: unknown
text: "By your patron?"

speaker: narrator
text: He kept his eyes on the wrap.

speaker: narrator
text: She tilted the coin; the frost vanished as if swallowed.

speaker: unknown
text: "When they call, I go. And right now I'm held together with spite and linen. If I cast like I did yesterday, I'll lose these."

speaker: narrator
text: She lifted the gloved hand. He knew what was under it.

speaker: unknown
text: "Next time it's the hand. Then the arm."

speaker: narrator
text: He nodded once.

speaker: unknown
text: "Do you want me to tell the others?"

speaker: unknown
text: "In a bit."

speaker: narrator
text: She pressed a looped obsidian sliver into his palm -- smooth, black, the length of a thumbnail and sharp enough to draw blood from the air around it.

speaker: unknown
text: "One breath where lies land like truth. Spend it to buy time, not fate."

speaker: unknown
text: "What does that mean?"

speaker: unknown
text: "It means you'll know when you need it. And when you use it, you'll wish you hadn't. That's how all the best gifts work."

speaker: narrator
text: Before he could answer, she drew out a narrow dark ribbon knotted with a hair-fine thread of silver.

speaker: unknown
text: "Tie it under your sleeve. If I'm within about a hundred miles, the silver will shade darker. Not a map -- just nearness."

speaker: narrator
text: He studied it.

speaker: unknown
text: "That helps."

speaker: narrator
text: She didn't add -- and he didn't ask -- that the knot carried a return thread only she could read; that if she needed to find him, the ribbon answered her too. Some gifts are best given without explaining the price tag.

speaker: unknown
text: "Anything else I should know?"

speaker: narrator
text: he asked.

speaker: unknown
text: "Only this."

speaker: narrator
text: She took his wrist, quick and careful, and tied the ribbon for him beneath the wrap where it wouldn't show. Her fingers -- even the dark ones -- moved with the precise grace of someone who'd learned that dexterity was not optional in her profession.

speaker: unknown
text: "I'm going because I must, not because I want to. And -- "

speaker: narrator
text: her grin flashed, wicked even through exhaustion

speaker: unknown
text: " -- try not to exhaust yourself without me. I'd hate to miss it."

speaker: narrator
text: Even hurt, he huffed a laugh.

speaker: unknown
text: "No promises."

speaker: unknown
text: "Good boy."

speaker: narrator
text: Two fingers -- the least ruined -- touched his cheek, almost ceremonial. A blessing from a woman who didn't believe in blessings, offered to a man who didn't believe he deserved one.

speaker: unknown
text: "Walk with ghosts if you must. Don't become one to keep them company."

speaker: narrator
text: She stepped away into the lane, her boots finding the cleanest path through the scorched cobbles without apparent effort. She didn't look back. She was good at not looking back. She'd had a lot of practice.

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin watched her go. The yard felt larger -- the way a room feels larger when the heat has gone, when the particular energy of a person who takes up more space than their body occupies simply isn't there anymore.

speaker: narrator
text: He touched the ribbon under his wrap. The silver thread was cool. He wondered how far a hundred miles was and whether he'd feel the moment it went dark.

speaker: narrator
text: The morning after a siege sounds like a town remembering how to breathe. Somewhere a hammer worked at timber -- the gate Thornik had repaired, being reinforced now by the local smith with less skill and more nails.

speaker: narrator
text: A child cried, then stopped -- comforted or exhausted, impossible to tell. The smell of fresh bread moved through smoke-tinged air from a bakehouse that had stayed lit through everything, because bread doesn't care about thralls.

speaker: narrator
text: Across the yard, a woman was sweeping the square's south corner with the focused determination of someone who had decided that if she could make one small section of the world clean, the rest would follow. The broom moved in short, fierce strokes.

speaker: narrator
text: She didn't look at the scorch marks. She swept around them.

speaker: narrator
text: They were checking tack and repacking what hadn't burned in the stable yard when Caelin said,

speaker: caelin
text: "Nyx was summoned. She left word that she doesn't know when -- or if -- she'll be back."

speaker: narrator
text: The words landed in different ways on different people.

speaker: narrator
text: Vex set down a saddlebag with more care than usual -- her ribs announcing themselves with every twist.

speaker: caelin
text: "Wonderful. Fewer purple fireworks and unsolicited philosophy."

speaker: narrator
text: She pulled a strap tight and checked the buckle twice. A beat passed. Softer, grudging:

speaker: caelin
text: "Also fewer brain-scrambles when death squads show up, so... I suppose I'll miss the asset. A little."

speaker: narrator
text: She slid a dagger home.

speaker: caelin
text: "Don't tell her I said that."

speaker: narrator
text: Thornik scratched his beard with a knuckle, eyes on the middle distance.

speaker: caelin
text: "Wouldn't dare. She'd charge us for sentiment."

speaker: narrator
text: Serana only nodded, eyes already on the road that led east. She'd been looking at it since first light. Not longingly -- assessingly. The way you look at a road you know leads somewhere you need to go and are afraid to arrive.

speaker: caelin
text: "We keep moving."

speaker: narrator
text: Durgan said nothing. He stood at the yard's edge, blades already cleaned and sheathed, shadow flat and obedient at his feet in the morning sun. If the news moved him, it moved him in a place no one could see.

speaker: narrator
text: But his eyes went once to the lane where Nyxara had walked, and something in his face -- not expression, exactly, but the ghost of an expression -- suggested he understood leaving better than most.

speaker: narrator
text: They worked in quiet for a while -- the productive, necessary quiet of people packing for a journey they're not sure they're coming back from. Rolled blankets, counted coin, checked the edge on steel that had been used the night before and would be used again.

speaker: narrator
text: Across the yard, Aldric was inventorying his medical bag with the thorough patience of someone who'd learned the cost of discovering a shortage at the wrong moment. Needle count. Thread count. Three types of bandage, rolled tight as coin stacks.

speaker: narrator
text: A leather pouch of dried herbs he could identify by smell with his eyes closed, and did, because the lighting was poor and he trusted his nose more than his eyes.

speaker: narrator
text: He didn't participate in the conversation. He listened the way old soldiers listen -- head down, hands busy, cataloguing everything. The way they moved. The way they talked. What they said and what they didn't.

speaker: narrator
text: He was assembling a picture of this group the way he assembled a trauma assessment: systematically, without judgment, noting what worked and what was about to fail.

speaker: narrator
text: What he saw: a party that had been seven and was now six and carried the weight of seven. A hedge-knight with a relic in his arm and a sense of duty that would get him killed if nobody watched.

speaker: narrator
text: A thief who fought like a soldier and cared like a mother and would deny both under oath. A dwarf whose heart was too big for his chest and whose toolkit was too empty for his skills. A paladin whose faith had broken and whose spine had not.

speaker: narrator
text: A shadow-cursed soldier who was losing a fight nobody else could see. And -- watching from the gap Nyxara had left -- an empty space where a warlock with darkening fingers had stood, filled now with morning light and absence.

speaker: narrator
text: He'd seen worse groups in better shape. He'd seen better groups in worse shape. He'd work with what was there.

speaker: narrator
text: They found Thornik behind the smithy, where the local smith had let him use the corner of a workbench.

speaker: narrator
text: He'd laid his tools out in a row -- not randomly, but the way a soldier lays out equipment for inspection, each item in its place, each absence accounted for. The empty charge-loops from his harness. A cracked lens.

speaker: narrator
text: The housing for a device that no longer had a mechanism to house. The things that had survived the mountain and the siege and could be carried forward. The things that couldn't.

speaker: narrator
text: Between them, like a border marker between countries, lay the dragon-dwarf chisel -- the one from his grandfather's maps, the etchings worn to suggestion, the kind of thing you had to know was there to see.

speaker: caelin
text: "I'm leaving,"

speaker: narrator
text: he said, without looking up. The words came out heavier than he'd perhaps intended, landing on the stone floor of the smithy like something dropped.

speaker: caelin
text: "Not because I can't scrape up gear in Millbrook. I could. There's a metalworker on the east lane who does passable work, and I've made do with less."

speaker: narrator
text: He picked up the chisel and turned it in his hands.

speaker: narrator
text: The firelight caught the faint etchings -- dwarven script so old it predated the current alphabet, letters that curved where modern runes angled, from an age when the clans still wrote in circles because straight lines were considered presumptuous.

speaker: caelin
text: "I'm leaving because I have to stand in front of my clan and say my grandfather wasn't mad. He was right."

speaker: narrator
text: He set the chisel down and began wrapping it in oilcloth, slowly, with the care of someone performing a small ceremony -- the kind you invent because the real ceremonies were lost with the people who used to perform them.

speaker: caelin
text: "His journals, his maps, the chisel -- if I don't put them in Deepstone hands myself, he stays a punchline and my name goes with him. That matters where I come from. It matters to me."

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin watched him wrap the chisel. He thought about the mountain, about the vault that had opened for Thornik's key and no one else's. About a dead man's journal written in blood and a living grandson who'd carried it through fire.

speaker: narrator
text: He thought about what it costs to be right for thirty years in a room full of people who think you're wrong.

speaker: thornik
text: "I'll retool at home,"

speaker: narrator
text: Thornik continued.

speaker: caelin
text: "Proper charges, lenses that don't crack when you look at them sideways, a housing that doesn't rattle like a drunk uncle at a wedding."

speaker: narrator
text: He almost smiled.

speaker: thornik
text: "But that's not the reason. The reason is lineage. And truth. And a stubborn old dwarf who deserved better than a footnote."

speaker: narrator
text: He turned to each of them, and this part he'd clearly thought about -- each gift chosen with the careful precision of a man who'd spent his life measuring things and knew exactly what each person needed.

speaker: narrator
text: To Vex, he held out a slim leather case, small enough to disappear into a palm. She opened it: three smoke-sticks, the kind that went off on impact and made it hard to see anything for thirty seconds.

speaker: caelin
text: "Disappearing, not winning,"

speaker: narrator
text: he said.

speaker: thornik
text: "Don't be clever with them. Just go."

speaker: vex
text: "You've met me,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex said.

speaker: thornik
text: "That's why I only gave you three."

speaker: narrator
text: He held her gaze.

speaker: vex
text: "One for each time you should have run and didn't."

speaker: narrator
text: She tucked them away without looking at them, which was how Vex showed she was moved.

speaker: narrator
text: To Caelin, an iron ring the width of a thumb, one quadrant filed to a gap. It sat heavy in the palm -- heavier than iron had a right to be, warm from Thornik's hands.

speaker: thornik
text: "At dusk on Sixthday, tap three-one-two on any stone. If I'm above ground and within a week's march, it'll hum toward me."

speaker: narrator
text: He pressed it into Caelin's palm.

speaker: vex
text: "Don't lose it. I only made two and the other one's mine."

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin turned the ring. The gap caught the light.

speaker: thornik
text: "How does it work?"

speaker: vex
text: "Resonance. The same principle as the chasm bridge -- sympathetic vibration across a tuned frequency. Mine and yours ring at the same pitch. Doesn't matter how much stone is between them."

speaker: narrator
text: He shrugged, as if the engineering of a device that could locate a specific person across a mountain range was roughly as complicated as building a birdhouse.

speaker: thornik
text: "Dwarf work. We're good at making things that find each other."

speaker: narrator
text: To Serana, last, a gear-coin stamped with the Deepstone mark -- a small bronze disc the size of a knuckle, the clan symbol cut so fine it caught fingerprints like a mirror.

speaker: vex
text: "If I go quiet too long, show this at any Deepstone hall. They'll know how to find me."

speaker: narrator
text: He held her gaze.

speaker: thornik
text: "I'm not planning to go quiet."

speaker: vex
text: "I know,"

speaker: narrator
text: she said, and took the coin with both hands -- the way you take something from someone when you want them to feel you taking it.

speaker: thornik
text: "I hate leaving you,"

speaker: narrator
text: he finished, and his voice went rough at the edges in the way of people who don't usually let it.

speaker: narrator
text: The gruff warmth that had carried them through cold camps and bad tunnels and worse food -- the voice that had told stories about cousins with independent eyebrows and made tea from minerals that should not have been drinkable -- cracked, just once, and then steadied.

speaker: vex
text: "But if I stay, I'm another body with the wrong tools. If I go, I come back with the right kit and proof that my grandfather's name means something. And he stops laughing alone."

speaker: narrator
text: Vex tucked the smoke-case into her jacket.

speaker: thornik
text: "Try not to die of politics."

speaker: vex
text: "No promises,"

speaker: narrator
text: he said, and almost smiled.

speaker: thornik
text: "You know how clans are."

speaker: narrator
text: They found her by the half-fallen chapel arch, alone. The chapel had taken smoke damage on the south wall; one window was gone, and the pale morning light came through the gap in a way that made the remaining stone look older than it was.

speaker: narrator
text: She stood with her fingers resting on the arch, not in prayer -- she'd have been the first to say so -- but in the way of someone reading a language they used to be fluent in and were not sure they still spoke.

speaker: narrator
text: The stone was cool under her fingertips.

speaker: narrator
text: She could feel the mason's work -- the precision of the cuts, the way each block had been fitted to its neighbor with the care of someone who believed that straight lines and right angles were their own form of worship.

speaker: narrator
text: Once, she'd felt the divine in stones like these. Now she felt the geometry and the intent, and neither of those were gods. They were craft. They were beautiful. But they weren't the voice she'd spent fifteen years following.

speaker: narrator
text: She heard them approach and didn't turn.

speaker: vex
text: "I've been trying to find a way to say this that isn't a failure,"

speaker: narrator
text: she said.

speaker: thornik
text: "I haven't found one. So I'll just say it."

speaker: narrator
text: She turned then. Her face was composed -- the particular composure of someone who'd made a decision and was standing behind it, not because it was easy but because it was the only honest thing left.

speaker: vex
text: "My light failed,"

speaker: narrator
text: she said.

speaker: thornik
text: "You've seen it. It may return -- I don't know -- but right now I can't pray with any certainty that someone is listening. And I can't lead you while I don't know whose voice I'm following, if any."

speaker: narrator
text: She let that sit. It deserved the space.

speaker: vex
text: "That would be dishonest. And dishonest faith is worse than no faith at all -- it gets people killed on promises you can't keep."

speaker: narrator
text: The silence after that was the kind that asks not to be filled quickly.

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin remembered the forked whisper -- Shield the bearer. Offer the bearer.

speaker: narrator
text: He'd never heard it himself, but he'd seen its effect on Serana's face: the particular horror of someone who'd discovered that the voice they trusted most might be two things wearing one name. He didn't ask about it now.

speaker: narrator
text: She'd tell them when she was ready, or she wouldn't, and either way it was hers.

speaker: thornik
text: "Where will you go?"

speaker: narrator
text: he asked.

speaker: vex
text: "East. There's a place -- an old house of retreat, not affiliated with any order -- where they take people who are having difficulty with questions like mine."

speaker: narrator
text: A pause.

speaker: thornik
text: "People who have lost the signal and are trying to find it again. Or learning to live without it."

speaker: narrator
text: She looked at the gap in the chapel wall, where the morning light poured through the absence like a answer to a question nobody had asked.

speaker: vex
text: "I don't know which of those I'm doing yet."

speaker: thornik
text: "How long?"

speaker: vex
text: "I don't know that either."

speaker: narrator
text: She almost smiled.

speaker: thornik
text: "Which is, in itself, a kind of answer."

speaker: narrator
text: She unrolled a strip of plain linen inked with a knotwork sigil -- old work, careful, the lines laid down with a steady hand that had not trembled.

speaker: vex
text: "A vigil-knot. No goddess required -- just breath and intention. Tie it round a wrist, speak the count of nine slowly, and it holds a line against panic for about an hour. Not much."

speaker: narrator
text: She almost smiled.

speaker: thornik
text: "Enough, sometimes."

speaker: narrator
text: She pressed one into Caelin's hand, then set three more on the stone ledge of the arch.

speaker: vex
text: "For whoever needs them. They'll keep."

speaker: narrator
text: Thornik took one without being asked, wrapping it around his thick wrist like a bracelet.

speaker: thornik
text: "Panic and I are old friends,"

speaker: narrator
text: he said.

speaker: vex
text: "We have an arrangement. But backup never hurts."

speaker: serana
text: "If I'm meant to come back, I will,"

speaker: narrator
text: Serana said.

speaker: vex
text: "And if not -- "

speaker: narrator
text: her eyes moved to each of them in turn, settling last on Caelin

speaker: serana
text: " -- you already know how to stand. You've been doing it since the mountain. You just didn't notice because I was nearby."

speaker: narrator
text: Vex had been quiet the whole time, which was unusual enough to notice. She unfolded her arms -- carefully, because of the rib -- and said:

speaker: vex
text: "If your god is taking interviews, tell Her the customer service is abysmal."

speaker: narrator
text: The ghost of a smile crossed Serana's face -- real, not performed. The kind that comes when someone gives you exactly the thing you need, and the thing you need turns out to be a joke.

speaker: serana
text: "I'll pass it along."

speaker: vex
text: "Also tell Her,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex continued,

speaker: vex
text: "that you were the best paladin I've met, and I've met four, and three of them were idiots. You were the one who wasn't. So if She's got any sense, She'll pick up the damn prayer and answer it."

speaker: narrator
text: Serana's composure cracked, just once, just briefly -- the flash of emotion underneath the armor, the person beneath the title. She blinked once, steadied.

speaker: serana
text: "Thank you."

speaker: vex
text: "Don't thank me. I'll deny saying it."

speaker: narrator
text: Serana picked up her pack. It was lighter than it had been. She'd left the heavy things behind -- and the light things, too. She'd left everything that wasn't her.

speaker: narrator
text: They met at the mile-stone where the road forked into three good choices and a dozen bad ones. The stone had names carved into it, most worn past reading -- travellers who'd wanted to leave something behind at a crossing.

speaker: narrator
text: Woodsmoke rode the evening air from the town, thinner now, dying down to its ordinary register.

speaker: vex
text: "Three roads,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex said.

speaker: serana
text: "How poetic."

speaker: narrator
text: No one contradicted her.

speaker: narrator
text: Thornik had his pack on his back and his grandfather's chisel wrapped and stowed and his tool-harness sitting lighter than it had in months -- not because it was empty but because what remained in it was what he'd chosen to keep.

speaker: narrator
text: He looked north toward the mountains and then back at the group with the expression of a man who has made his decision and is waiting to see how it feels to live with it.

speaker: serana
text: "Three roads,"

speaker: narrator
text: Serana answered, thumb working the edge of the gear-coin Thornik had given her.

speaker: vex
text: "I don't know when mine turns back toward yours."

speaker: thornik
text: "With luck,"

speaker: narrator
text: Thornik said, and he tapped Caelin's tone-ring against the stone -- three-one-two, a sound like a small careful knock --

speaker: vex
text: "mine will rejoin before Depthspire. If not, the ring knows the way."

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin pressed the ring between his fingers and felt the gap where the quadrant was missing.

speaker: narrator
text: He thought about what it meant that Thornik had kept the matching one -- that somewhere, in a dwarven hall under a mountain, a stubborn engineer would tap three-one-two on Sixthday dusk and wait for the hum that meant his friends were still alive.

speaker: narrator
text: He tightened the dark ribbon under his sleeve and said:

speaker: thornik
text: "We don't wait at doors we can't close. If we have to go in, we go in."

speaker: serana
text: "And we find each other if we can,"

speaker: narrator
text: Serana said.

speaker: narrator
text: They stood at the stone for a moment longer than was strictly necessary. Nobody was in a rush to be the first to walk away.

speaker: narrator
text: The light was going copper at the edges and the wind had come up from the northwest, carrying the smell of distance -- cold grass, bare earth, the particular nothing-smell of a road that goes somewhere you haven't been.

speaker: narrator
text: They didn't hug. They weren't that kind of story yet. But they stood close enough that if any one of them had stumbled in that moment, the others would have caught them without thinking.

speaker: narrator
text: Thornik turned toward the northern road, the mountains catching the first cold stars. Serana took the eastward track, where old pilgrim stones lined the ditch like teeth worn to stubs. Caelin, Vex, and Durgan faced the merchant way.

speaker: narrator
text: The wind shifted. Iron-sweet from the ruined gate. Last hearth-smoke from a town that had held as long as it could. Somewhere far below stone, the Depthspire pulse came through the soles of their boots: Thud. Thud. Thud. Patient as geology. Counting.

speaker: narrator
text: They walked into their separate futures.

speaker: narrator
text: Aldric was waiting at the first bend in the road, medical bag over one shoulder, already facing north. He didn't say anything when they reached him. He fell into step at the back of the group, and that was that.

speaker: narrator
text: They walked for an hour before anyone spoke.

speaker: narrator
text: The silence wasn't hostile. It was the particular quiet of a group that had been seven and was now four and was still learning the shape of itself.

speaker: narrator
text: Every absence had a weight -- not grief exactly, but the phantom sensation of something that should have been there.

speaker: narrator
text: The space where Thornik's voice would have filled the gap with a story about his cousin or an observation about load-bearing architecture. The space where Serana would have set the marching order and nobody would have argued.

speaker: narrator
text: The space where Nyxara would have said something sharp and precise and exactly wrong in a way that turned out to be exactly right.

speaker: narrator
text: Even Elowen's absence had a texture -- a quiet where quiet used to mean someone was listening, and now just meant nobody was there.

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin caught himself glancing left for Thornik's click-and-whir, and then remembered. He checked over his shoulder for Serana's armored tread, and found only Durgan's silence and Aldric's unhurried gait.

speaker: narrator
text: Vex walked beside him, moving carefully -- her ribs still protesting the existence of ribcages -- and read the change in his face without comment. She'd been doing her own inventory. Three fewer blades in the formation. No charges left in any belt.

speaker: narrator
text: No divine light, borrowed or otherwise. No warlock tricks. No druid to ease the burn.

speaker: thornik
text: "We're thinner,"

speaker: narrator
text: she said eventually, because someone had to.

speaker: caelin
text: "We're faster,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said, because it was true and because the alternative was admitting they were weaker.

speaker: aldric
text: "We're both,"

speaker: narrator
text: Aldric said from the back, the first words he'd spoken since the bend.

speaker: caelin
text: "Thinner means fewer targets. Faster means harder to pin. In my experience, four people who trust each other are worth more than seven who don't. The question is whether you four trust each other."

speaker: vex
text: "Three,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex corrected, without looking back.

speaker: caelin
text: "You're new."

speaker: vex
text: "I am. And I'll earn it or I won't, and either way I'll keep your ribs taped and your fevers down. Trust can wait. Medicine can't."

speaker: narrator
text: Vex made a sound that might have been approval, or might have been her rib reminding her not to snort.

speaker: narrator
text: Durgan walked rear guard, the way he always walked -- silent, watchful, reading the hedgerows and ditch-lines for threat. His shadow moved with him, obedient, leashed. The nosebleed had stopped an hour ago.

speaker: narrator
text: He was counting the hours until the next one. The mathematics of his condition had simplified over the past week: the episodes were closer together, the visions more detailed, the shadow's reach longer.

speaker: narrator
text: The line between Durgan and the thing in his shadow was getting shorter, and he couldn't tell anymore whether he was holding the leash or wearing it.

speaker: narrator
text: He said none of this. He walked, and watched, and made the choice to keep walking.

speaker: narrator
text: The scale pulsed its steady coal-glow beneath Caelin's wrap. The same direction it had been pointing since the mountain. The same patient pull. The mission hadn't changed. The mission didn't care how many people carried it -- it cared that someone did.

speaker: narrator
text: At a stream crossing, Aldric knelt and filled the water skins with the efficient movements of someone who'd been provisioning for marches since before most of them were born. He checked the water's clarity, smelled it, tasted a drop, and nodded.

speaker: narrator
text: When he stood, he fell back into his position at the rear without being told, as if the walking order had been decided by someone and he'd simply read the diagram.

speaker: caelin
text: "You've done this before,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said.

speaker: aldric
text: "For twenty-five years,"

speaker: narrator
text: Aldric said.

speaker: caelin
text: "Different people. Different roads. Same direction."

speaker: narrator
text: He adjusted his bag's strap.

speaker: aldric
text: "The plateau has been waiting. I've been walking toward it. Whatever door opens at the far end -- "

speaker: narrator
text: He paused, chose his words.

speaker: caelin
text: "I've already seen what comes through when seals break. Thirty-seven men's worth of knowledge on the subject. I'd rather use that knowledge than carry it to a grave."

speaker: narrator
text: The road bent northeast. The land rose. The sky stretched clean and cold ahead of them, pale as bone, and somewhere beyond the visible horizon the Depthspire plateau waited with the patience of stone that had been counting heartbeats for a very long time.

speaker: narrator
text: Four people. One road. The wind tasted of iron and distance.

speaker: narrator
text: Thud. Thud. Thud. Patient as geology.

speaker: narrator
text: They walked.
